Copyright-Free Music for YouTube: The Best Free and Royalty-Free Sources (2026)

The safest free and royalty-free music for YouTube, plus the part most lists skip: how Content ID really works, why "no copyright" tracks from random channels still get claimed, and how to add music to any video without a copyright strike.

Published by Denzil

Updated June 15, 2026 · 13 min read

The fastest way to get a copyright claim on YouTube is to grab a "no copyright" track from a random channel and drop it into your video. A huge share of those uploads are re-uploads the channel never owned, so the moment you publish, Content ID matches the real rightsholder and claims your video anyway. Copyright-free music only works when it comes from a source that genuinely cleared the track.

So here is the safe short answer. For YouTube specifically, the YouTube Audio Library is the most reliable free source, and Pixabay Music and Uppbeat are the strongest free sites beyond it. This guide covers all of them, the paid libraries worth paying for, the AI options, and the part most articles skip: exactly how YouTube copyright works and how to add music to any video without a claim or a strike.

Copyright-Free vs Royalty-Free vs Public Domain

These terms get used interchangeably, and the confusion is what gets creators claimed. Here is what each one means:

  • Royalty-free does not mean free. It means you pay once (or nothing) and owe no ongoing per-play royalties. The track is still copyrighted; you simply hold a license to use it. This is the category most "free music for YouTube" sites fall into.
  • Copyright-free and no copyright are marketing phrases, not legal categories. They usually mean royalty-free or free-to-use, but the label alone guarantees nothing. Always check the actual license.
  • Public domain means the copyright has expired or been waived, so the work is genuinely free for anyone. Note the catch: a Beethoven composition is public domain, but a specific modern recording of it is not.
  • Creative Commons is a set of licenses with different rules. CC0 is free with no attribution, CC-BY is free but you must credit the artist, and CC-BY-NC is for non-commercial use only, which means you cannot use it on a monetized channel.

The practical takeaway: "free to download" is not the same as "free to use on a monetized YouTube video." The license is what matters, and we flag the attribution rules for every source below.

How YouTube Copyright Works

Two systems catch music on YouTube, and they are not the same thing. Understanding the difference tells you how much trouble you are really in.

Content ID is the automated one. It scans every upload against a massive database of registered audio, and a match places a Content ID claim on your video, often within minutes, even if you used only a few seconds. A claim is not a punishment. Depending on what the rightsholder chose, it can run ads on your video with the revenue going to them, mute the audio, or block the video in some countries. Your channel is not penalized.

A copyright strike is the serious one. It is a manual legal takedown request from a rightsholder, and three strikes in 90 days terminates your channel. Using cleared copyright-free music protects you from both, but the distinction matters: a claim costs you the video's ad revenue, while a strike threatens the whole channel.

Key point: a Content ID claim is not a copyright strike. If a track you licensed gets a false claim, you do not lose anything by disputing it with your license as proof. Claims are common and reversible; strikes are rare and dangerous.

The YouTube Audio Library: The Safest Free Source

If you only use one source, use this one. The YouTube Audio Library is built into YouTube Studio (under the Audio Library tab), it is completely free, and every track in it is cleared to use on YouTube without triggering a Content ID claim. You can filter by genre, mood, instrument, and duration, and most tracks need no attribution, though a small number are CC-BY and ask for a credit, which Studio flags on each track. It also includes a free sound effects library, so you can pull both music and SFX from the same safe place.

Do not confuse it with Creator Music, which is a separate marketplace inside Studio offering popular, recognizable songs. Those are licensed per use (you either pay upfront or share revenue), unlike the Audio Library, which is free with no revenue share. One caveat worth knowing: Audio Library tracks are cleared for YouTube, not everywhere, so the same track can still get flagged if you repost the video to Instagram or TikTok.

The Best Free Royalty-Free Music Sites

Beyond the Audio Library, these are the free sources worth bookmarking. The attribution column tells you whether you must credit the artist to stay licensed.

SourceLibrary sizeAttributionBest for
Pixabay Music30,000+NoneFast, safe, no-credit downloads
Uppbeat15,000+Credit on free tierChannel safelisting against claims
Mixkit1,000+NoneSimple commercial-use tracks
Free Music Archive180,000+Varies per trackIndie and experimental
Incompetech2,000+CC-BY (credit)Mood-based, deep catalog
Bensound600+Credit on free tierCorporate and explainer
FreePD & MusopenPublic domainNonePublic-domain and classical
ChosicAggregatorVariesSearching across many libraries

For most creators, Pixabay Music covers the no-attribution need and Uppbeat covers everything else, especially because Uppbeat lets you safelist your channel so its free tracks will not get claimed. Reach for the others when you want a specific mood or genre the first two do not carry.

Pro tip: before you commit to a track, search its title on YouTube. If dozens of videos already use it, yours will sound generic and the song carries a higher chance of a mistaken claim. The deeper, less-famous corners of these libraries are where you find music that makes a video feel custom rather than stock.

The Best No-Copyright Music YouTube Channels

Plenty of creators pull music straight from YouTube channels that publish free tracks, and a few are genuinely reliable. The well-known ones are NoCopyrightSounds (NCS), Vlog No Copyright Music, Audio Library, Music for Content Creators, and TeknoAxe. Most of these require you to credit the track and artist in your description, exactly as the channel specifies.

Watch out: this is where most claims happen. Many "no copyright" channels re-upload music they do not own, so when you use their version, Content ID still matches the real rightsholder and claims your video. Always grab the track from the original artist's official channel or library, follow their exact credit instructions, and never assume a channel's name means the music is genuinely cleared.
Cleared audio is one box to tick. Find the rest.A free Channel Audit reviews your channel for the issues quietly capping your growth, from metadata to packaging, so you fix the things that move views, not just the ones that avoid claims.Run a free Channel Audit →

Paid Royalty-Free Libraries (and When to Upgrade)

Free music is enough to start, but once you publish often or post to several platforms, a paid subscription pays for itself in time saved and claims avoided, and for a channel you run as a business, that reliability is worth paying for. These libraries license a huge catalog and, crucially, let you safelist your channels so their tracks never get claimed.

LibraryPrice (from)What you get
Epidemic Sound~$15/month50,000+ tracks, channel linking clears claims
Artlist~$16/monthLifetime license on anything you download while subscribed
Soundstripe~$12/monthStrong catalog with editor integrations
Storyblocks & Envato Elements~$15/monthMusic bundled with stock video, graphics, and templates

The signal that it is time to upgrade is simple: you are publishing more than one video a week, posting across platforms, or tired of recognizable free tracks. If that is you, the cross-platform claim protection alone is worth the subscription. If you upload occasionally, the free sources above are plenty.

AI Music Generators

A newer option in 2026 is generating your own track from a text prompt. Tools like Mubert, Suno, Udio, and MusicGPT can produce original background music in seconds, which sidesteps Content ID entirely because the track did not exist before you made it. For background ambiance, B-roll, and quick fills, this is a genuinely useful shortcut.

Two honest caveats. The commercial licensing of AI music is still unsettled, with active legal questions around some generators, so read each tool's license carefully before you monetize a video built on it. And AI tracks still trail human-composed music on emotional depth, so they suit background use more than a video where the music carries the moment. Treat them as a supplement, not a wholesale replacement for the libraries above.

How to Use Any Track Without a Copyright Claim

Whatever source you choose, these steps keep you on the safe side. Run through them before you publish, not after a claim appears.

  1. Default to the YouTube Audio Library for YouTube videos. It is pre-cleared, so nothing in it will claim your video on YouTube, which removes the guesswork entirely for the platform that matters most to you.
  2. Download from the official source, never a re-upload. Get the track from the original artist or the library that licensed it, because a second-hand "no copyright" copy is the single most common cause of a claim.
  3. Read the license before you use it. Confirm it allows commercial use, YouTube use, and monetization specifically. "Free to download" does not always include all three.
  4. Add the exact attribution required. If the license asks for a credit, paste the artist's specified text into your description word for word, because a missing or wrong credit voids the license.
  5. Keep your proof. Save the license PDF or the download page, so if a false claim ever appears you can dispute it in minutes with evidence.
  6. Use channel safelisting where offered. Libraries like Uppbeat and Epidemic Sound let you link your channel so their tracks never trigger a claim in the first place.

Music for Shorts, Vlogs, and Specific Genres

A few situations need their own approach. For Shorts, YouTube's in-app music picker is cleared for Shorts, but be careful: adding a popular commercial song to a Short can block that video from earning, so for a Short you want monetized, use Audio Library or royalty-free tracks instead, then lean on the Shorts algorithm guide to get it seen. For vlogs, the upbeat, low-key tracks on Uppbeat and Pixabay are the staple, and most libraries let you filter by exactly that mood.

By genre, the libraries above cover the common requests well: lofi and study beats (a favorite for faceless channels), cinematic scores, gaming and electronic, and seasonal sets like Christmas music. Filter by genre on Pixabay, Uppbeat, or the Audio Library first. And remember the cross-platform rule: a track cleared by the YouTube Audio Library can still get flagged on Instagram or TikTok, so if you repurpose the same video, use a library with multi-platform clearance.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the best copyright-free music for YouTube?

For YouTube specifically, the YouTube Audio Library is the safest free source, because every track is pre-cleared and will not trigger a Content ID claim on the platform. Beyond it, Pixabay Music (no attribution) and Uppbeat (with channel safelisting) are the strongest free options. For a deeper catalog and cross-platform claim protection, a paid library like Epidemic Sound or Artlist is worth the subscription.

Is royalty-free music really free?

Not always. Royalty-free means you owe no ongoing per-play royalties, not that the track costs nothing. Many royalty-free sites do offer free downloads, but some require a credit or a paid plan, and a few are free only for non-commercial use. Always check the license before using a track on a monetized video.

Do I have to credit the artist?

It depends on the license. CC0 and most YouTube Audio Library tracks need no credit, while CC-BY tracks and most free-tier downloads from sites like Uppbeat, Bensound, and NCS require you to paste the artist's specified attribution into your description. Skipping a required credit voids the license and can still get your video claimed, so when in doubt, add the credit.

Can I monetize a video that uses free music?

Yes, as long as the track is cleared for commercial use and monetization. The YouTube Audio Library is pre-cleared for monetized videos, and most reputable royalty-free libraries allow it too. Avoid CC-BY-NC tracks, which are non-commercial only, and always confirm the license covers monetization rather than just personal use.

Why did I get a copyright claim on royalty-free music?

Usually because the track was downloaded from a re-upload channel rather than the original source, so Content ID matched the real rightsholder. It can also happen when a legitimate library has not whitelisted your channel, which produces a false claim you can dispute with your license. Download from official sources and keep your proof to avoid and resolve these.

What is the difference between a copyright claim and a copyright strike?

A Content ID claim is automated and not a penalty: it can place ads, mute, or block the video, but your channel is fine. A copyright strike is a manual legal takedown, and three strikes in 90 days terminates your channel. Copyright-free music protects you from both, but a claim is a minor revenue issue while a strike is a serious threat.

Pick the Right Source, Skip the Claim

Copyright-free music for YouTube is not complicated once you stop trusting the label and start trusting the source. The YouTube Audio Library is your default, Pixabay and Uppbeat fill the gaps, a paid library earns its keep once you scale, and AI fills in the background, all of it safe as long as you take the track from a source that genuinely cleared it.

Read the license, add the credit when it is asked for, keep your proof, and never grab a track from a random "no copyright" channel. Do that, and the music stops being a risk and becomes what it should be: the part of the video you get to enjoy choosing, not the part that gets you claimed.
